items.q: What is a positive climate feedback loop?
items.correct: A process where warming triggers changes that cause more warming — e.g. melting Arctic ice exposes dark ocean, which absorbs more heat, melting more ice (ice-albedo feedback).
items.misconception: Positive feedback means a good outcome for the climate.
items.random_wrong: Positive feedback means the climate balances itself out.
items.why: 'Positive' here means amplifying, not beneficial. Negative feedbacks (which stabilise) are positive for us. The terminology trips students up — it refers to direction of change, not desirability.

items.q: What is the main social impact of gentrification?
items.correct: Displacement of long-term lower-income residents as rents and property prices rise, often along with loss of community businesses and cultural identity — even if the area looks 'improved'.
items.misconception: Gentrification only has positive effects because it makes areas nicer.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means evicting all residents by force.
items.why: Improvements aren't free — they're paid for by displacement. Students focus on visible upgrades and miss the demographic shift. Many evaluation questions ask 'for whom is gentrification beneficial?'.

items.q: What causes global sea level rise?
items.correct: Two main factors: thermal expansion of warming seawater, and the melting of land-based ice (glaciers and ice sheets) adding water to oceans.
items.misconception: Sea level rises mainly because Arctic sea ice is melting.
items.random_wrong: Sea level rises because of underwater volcanoes pushing it up.
items.why: Floating sea ice already displaces its weight in water — its melting does not raise sea level. The contributors are LAND ice (Greenland, Antarctica) and thermal expansion.

items.q: What population threshold defines a megacity?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: More than 10 million. “More than 1 million” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is the largest urban agglomeration?
items.why: Correct answer: Tokyo. “Mumbai” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many megacities exist worldwide today?
items.why: Exact answer: 30. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
